metadata.title: Solo Dev vs. The Engine: The True Cost of Building a Game Alone
metadata.color_semantics: claude fidelity: INK #3D3929; SPARK #D97757 one accent.
metadata.author: Seth Brown
tags: Solo Dev vs. The Engine: The True Cost o
metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Seth Brown — Humanitarians AI Fellow. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Even on an engine you still build the actual rooms and systems yourself, and that choice should be explicit. Eric Barone did not write Stardew's engine from scratch; he built on existing tools. Godot's MIT license means zero royalties, subscription fees, or hidden per-install costs. Godot became the indie darling of 2025 as developers left Unity after the 2023 pricing controversy.

artifactLines: Even on an engine you still build the actual rooms and systems yourself, and that choice should be explicit.
Eric Barone did not write Stardew's engine from scratch; he built on existing tools.
Godot's MIT license means zero royalties, subscription fees, or hidden per-install costs.
Godot became the indie darling of 2025 as developers left Unity after the 2023 pricing controversy.

beats.narration_text: I keep hearing that Eric Barone didn't build his engine from scratch, and that Godot won indie hearts after Unity's 2023 pricing mess. I want to make the true cost concrete for myself before I start. Compare building a small 2D game on Godot versus writing my own engine. Give me: (1) a task-by-task breakdown of what the engine hands me for free, (2) where the MIT/free license actually saves money, (3) the three hidden time costs of going engine-free.

output: A framework like SDL2 or MonoGame gives you a floor, not a house
But each one sets a trajectory, and trajectories compound over ye
The case for a custom engine rests on one claim: zero bloat
